title: "xorg.conf default settings"
date: 2008-09-12
forum: Intrepid Ibex Testing and Discussion (CLOSED)

### Post by nickwu2000 on 2008-09-12
I have installed ibex, and I've been having troubles installing my nvidia card.

Whilst mucking around, I wanted to reset my xorg.conf file. The entire file now looks like this:

```

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
EndSection
```

It seems to work fine, and I'm guessing it's using the nv drivers, and appropriate defaults -- I haven't defined Configured Monitor, Configured Video Device, or Default Screen. 

My question is this: where are the default settings coming from, and what are they?

---

### Post by Trespasser on 2008-09-12
Good question. I've wondered the same thing.

---

### Post by chrisccoulson on 2008-09-12
The capabilities and optimum settings for your screen are detected on-the-fly using information provided from your monitor. You don't need to statically configure screens anymore (this is how Hardy is too).

---

### Post by ronacc on 2008-09-12
go to system>administration>hardware drivers and it should show the nvidia drivers , enable the apropriate one for your card . then you should be using the nvidia driver . you can check if you are by installing nvidia-settings , it will show the # of your nvidia driver if it is running and tell you if it isn't .

---

### Post by autocrosser on 2008-09-12
I needed to modify my custom xorg.conf with the 1.5.0 Xorg update--It INSISTS that hal config the keyboard & mouse--you can change your screen & device still....

My now looks like:

# nvidia-settings: X configuration file generated by nvidia-settings
# nvidia-settings:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder3)  Mon Feb 26 23:39:38 PST 2007

Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Screen0"
    Device        "Videocard0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    Option        "metamodes"    "1920x1200_60; 1680x1050_60; 1600x1200_60; 1440x900_60; 1280x1024_75; 1024x768_75; 1024x768_60"
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth    24
        Modes        "1920x1200_60"    "1680x1050_60"    "1600x1200_60"    "1440x900_60"    "1280x1024_75"    "1280x1024_60"    "1024x768_75"    "1024x768_60"
    EndSubSection
    Defaultdepth    24
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Videocard0"
    Driver        "nvidia"
    Vendorname    "NVIDIA Corporation"
    Boardname    "GeForce 8600 GT"
        Option        "Coolbits"    "1"
        Option        "TripleBuffer"    "True"
    Option        "NoLogo"    "False"
    Option        "SLI"    "SFR"
    BusID    "3:0:0"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "Single-Monitor"
        Screen          "Screen0"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load        "dbe"
    Load        "extmod"
    Load        "int10"
    Load        "vbe"
    Load        "freetype"
    Load        "glx"
    Load        "bitmap"
    Load        "v4l"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    # HorizSync source: edid, VertRefresh source: edid
    Displaysize    593.28    370.8
    Identifier    "Monitor0"
    Vendorname    "HannsG"
    Modelname    "HG-281DPB"
    Horizsync    24.0    -    80.0
    Vertrefresh    56.0    -    75.0
    Option        "DPMS"    "true"
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
    Option        "Composite"    "Enable"
EndSection

I'm sure that I could remove the "Modules" section without any problem--I do define the Monitor due to a mis-read that still happens.

### Post by Nullack on 2008-09-12
Ack, autocrosser that conf is a bug report waiting to happen nightmare my friend :)

Heres mine:

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"NVIDIA Device"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	Option		"NoLogo"	"True"
EndSection

---

### Post by autocrosser on 2008-09-13
Might be true, but I don't get correct modes without the screen section & I run SLI, so there are more lines in the device area......I keep a close eye on Xorg info (on the developer-list) & have wrote xorg.conf(s) for 10+ years now--so I feel comfortable re-writing one on the fly--took me about 3~4 minutes to figure out that 1.5.0 won't run configured mouse/keyboard anymore--so I dropped to a term & fired up my old friend nano (I know. nano is so 80's :)  ).

I'll experiment to how little I can get away with over the next couple of days--need to ask some questions on the dev list & do some reading.....

### Post by autocrosser on 2008-09-13
Yes--I've used nano for what seems like 20 years or so--handy in a pinch--never liked vi very much...I know you can do "so" much more with vi, but I've not needed that kind of power in a long time (was using a UNIX system back then)

OK--in the interests of cleaning things up:

# nvidia-settings: X configuration file generated by nvidia-settings
# nvidia-settings:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder3)  Mon Feb 26 23:39:38 PST 2007

Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Screen0"
    Device        "Videocard0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    Option        "metamodes"    "1920x1200_60; 1680x1050_60; 1600x1200_60; 1440x900_60; 1280x1024_75; 1024x768_75; 1024x768_60"
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth    24
        Modes        "1920x1200_60"    "1680x1050_60"    "1600x1200_60"    "1440x900_60"    "1280x1024_75"    "1280x1024_60"    "1024x768_75"    "1024x768_60"
    EndSubSection
    Defaultdepth    24
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Videocard0"
    Driver        "nvidia"
    Vendorname    "NVIDIA Corporation"
    Boardname    "GeForce 8600 GT"
        Option        "Coolbits"    "1"
        Option        "TripleBuffer"    "True"
    Option        "NoLogo"    "False"
        Option          "PixmapCacheSize" "1000000"
        Option          "AllowSHMPixmaps" "0"
    Option        "SLI"    "SFR"
    BusID    "3:0:0"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "Single-Monitor"
        Screen          "Screen0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    # HorizSync source: edid, VertRefresh source: edid
    Displaysize    593.28    370.8
    Identifier    "Monitor0"
    Vendorname    "HannsG"
    Modelname    "HG-281DPB"
    Horizsync    24.0    -    80.0
    Vertrefresh    56.0    -    75.0
    Option        "DPMS"    "true"
EndSection


The two pixmap options are from the referenced nVidia thread--I also put the two nVidia-settings mod in my sessions startup--netted me about 400FPS extra.

I realized that I didn't reference the thread---  [http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=118088](http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=118088)
